authentication:
• Authentication is the process of verifying a user's identity before granting access.
• It ensures that the person logging in is genuinely who they claim to be.
authorization:
• Authorization determines what actions a user is allowed to perform after login.
• It controls access to resources based on roles and permissions.
difference between authentication and au:
• Authentication verifies identity, while authorization defines access rights.
• Both are essential for securing systems and managing user access.
username and password:
• A username identifies a user, while a password verifies their identity.
• Together, they form the most basic authentication method.
multi-factor authentication:
• MFA requires users to provide multiple verification factors.
• This significantly increases security by adding extra layers beyond passwords.
mfa:
• MFA requires users to provide multiple verification factors.
• This significantly increases security by adding extra layers beyond passwords.
two-factor authentication:
• 2FA is a type of MFA using exactly two verification methods.
• For example, a password plus an OTP sent to a mobile device.
2fa:
• 2FA is a type of MFA using exactly two verification methods.
• For example, a password plus an OTP sent to a mobile device.
session:
• A session is a temporary connection between a user and a system.
• It stores user data after login to maintain authentication state.
token:
• A token is a digital credential used to verify identity after login.
• It allows secure communication without repeatedly entering credentials.
login system:
• A login system allows users to authenticate and access applications.
• It verifies credentials and creates a secure session.
logout:
• Logout ends a user session and removes access to the system.
• It helps prevent unauthorized access when a user leaves.
password hashing:
• Password hashing converts passwords into unreadable strings.
• This protects them even if the database is compromised.
why should passwords be hashed:
• Hashed passwords cannot be easily reversed into plain text.
• This ensures user data remains secure even if leaked.
encryption:
• Encryption converts data into a coded format for security.
• Only authorized users can decode and access the information.
ssl/tls:
• SSL/TLS are protocols that secure internet communication.
• They encrypt data between users and servers.
user role:
• A user role defines a category of permissions.
• Examples include admin, user, and guest roles.
access control:
• Access control restricts who can view or use resources.
• It ensures only authorized users perform certain actions.
rbac:
• Role-Based Access Control assigns permissions based on roles.
• It simplifies managing access in large systems.
abac:
• Attribute-Based Access Control uses attributes like location or time.
• It provides more flexible and dynamic permission control.
permission:
• A permission defines what actions a user can perform.
• It can include read, write, update, or delete access.
protected route:
• A protected route is accessible only to authenticated users.
• It prevents unauthorized users from accessing sensitive pages.
oauth:
• OAuth is a protocol for delegated authorization.
• It allows apps to access user data without sharing passwords.
oauth 2.0:
• OAuth 2.0 is an improved and widely used authorization framework.
• It provides secure access using tokens instead of credentials.
openid connect:
• OpenID Connect is built on OAuth 2.0 for authentication.
• It helps verify user identity in modern applications.
jwt:
• JWT (JSON Web Token) is a compact authentication token.
• It securely transmits user data between client and server.
refresh token:
• A refresh token is used to generate new access tokens.
• It helps maintain user sessions without re-login.
access token:
• An access token allows users to access protected resources.
• It is usually short-lived for security purposes.
token expiration:
• Token expiration defines how long a token is valid.
• After expiry, users must re-authenticate.
bearer token:
• A bearer token grants access to whoever possesses it.
• It must be securely stored to prevent misuse.
sso:
• Single Sign-On allows users to log in once for multiple systems.
• It improves user experience and reduces password fatigue.
identity provider:
• An IdP authenticates users and provides identity data.
• Examples include Google or Microsoft login services.
idp:
• An IdP authenticates users and provides identity data.
• Examples include Google or Microsoft login services.
service provider:
• A service provider offers services to authenticated users.
• It relies on identity providers for user verification.
sp:
• A service provider offers services to authenticated users.
• It relies on identity providers for user verification.
brute force:
• A brute force attack tries many password combinations.
• It aims to guess credentials through repeated attempts.
phishing:
• Phishing tricks users into revealing sensitive information.
• It often uses fake emails or websites.
csrf:
• Cross-Site Request Forgery tricks users into unwanted actions.
• It exploits authenticated sessions without user consent.
xss:
• Cross-Site Scripting injects malicious scripts into websites.
• It can steal user data or session tokens.
password salting:
• Salting adds random data to passwords before hashing.
• It prevents attackers from using precomputed hash tables.
session hijacking:
• Session hijacking steals a user's active session.
• Attackers gain unauthorized access without login.
captcha:
• CAPTCHA verifies if a user is human.
• It prevents automated bots from abusing systems.
rate limiting:
• Rate limiting restricts the number of requests.
• It protects systems from abuse and attacks.
api authentication:
• API authentication verifies users accessing APIs.
• It ensures only authorized applications can interact.
api key:
• An API key is a unique identifier for API access.
• It is used to authenticate applications.
secret key:
• A secret key is used in encryption processes.
• It must be kept private to maintain security.
public key:
• A public key is shared openly for encryption.
• It works with a private key in cryptographic systems.
private key:
• A private key is kept secret for decryption.
• It ensures secure communication and data protection.
digital signature:
• A digital signature verifies data authenticity.
• It ensures data has not been altered.
zero trust:
• Zero trust means never trusting any user by default.
• Every request must be verified before access.
device authentication:
• Device authentication verifies trusted devices.
• It adds an extra security layer beyond users.
biometric:
• Biometric authentication uses fingerprints or face recognition.
• It provides strong and user-friendly security.
otp:
• OTP is a one-time password used for verification.
• It expires quickly to enhance security.
totp:
• TOTP is time-based OTP that expires quickly.
• It is commonly used in authentication apps.
login work:
• Users submit credentials which the server verifies.
• If valid, a session or token is created.
cookie-based:
• Authentication data is stored in browser cookies.
• It is commonly used in traditional web apps.
token-based:
• Authentication uses tokens instead of sessions.
• It is widely used in modern APIs and apps.
localstorage:
• Tokens are stored in browser localStorage.
• It persists even after browser restarts.
session storage:
• Session storage holds data temporarily in browser.
• It clears when the tab is closed.
middleware:
• Middleware checks authentication before processing requests.
• It protects routes and APIs.
backend authentication:
• Authentication handled on the server side.
• It ensures secure validation of user data.
frontend authentication:
• Authentication logic handled in UI.
• It improves user experience but relies on backend.
login api:
• An API endpoint for user authentication.
• It validates credentials and returns tokens.
logout api:
• An API that ends user sessions.
• It invalidates tokens or sessions.
